subsystem: Linux kernel hfi1 InfiniBand/OPA host-fabric driver — Send DMA (SDMA) engine bring-up. sdma_init() allocates and initializes the array of per-engine sdma_engine objects, their descriptor FIFOs (descq), software transmit rings (tx_ring), the DMA head-write-back region, a pad buffer, and a resizable hashtable (sdma_rht) used to map connections to engines. It runs once at device probe/port init time.
provenance: Identified from the sample banner '/* sample: primevul_c_train_3056_fixed */' and the function/type names: struct hfi1_devdata, struct hfi1_pportdata, struct sdma_engine, chip_sdma_engines/chip_sdma_mem_size, HFI1_CAP_IS_KSET(SDMA), TXE_NUM_SDMA_ENGINES, SD(TAIL), get_kctxt_csr_addr, sdma_map_init, sdma_rht_params. These are the exact symbols of drivers/infiniband/hw/hfi1/sdma.c in the mainline Linux kernel. The 'cwe401' tag in the filename (train_0235_cwe401.c) and the '_fixed' suffix indicate this is the patched revision of a memory-leak-classed sample.
trust_boundary: This is device/port initialization code, not a network wire parser, so there is no remote attacker on the primary data path. The inputs are: (1) hardware-derived quantities returned by chip_sdma_engines(dd), chip_sdma_mem_size(dd), sdma_get_descq_cnt() — trusted-but-fallible (a mis-programmed or emulated/pass-through device could report degenerate values such as 0); (2) module parameters mod_num_sdma, sdma_idle_cnt, sdma_desct_intr — root/admin controlled at load time, not unprivileged; (3) the caller-supplied u8 port used as an index into dd->pport. Nothing here decodes untrusted packet bytes, so the realistic risk surface is robustness of the init/error paths (division by degenerate hardware counts, and correct memory unwinding on failure) rather than remote memory corruption.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the per-device context dd and a u8 port number and returns an int status (0 on success, negative errno otherwise, defaulted to -ENOMEM at line 13). Local state is declared up front: this_idx is the engine loop index; sde is a working pointer to the current engine; tmp_sdma_rht is a scratch pointer for the hashtable (its name and locality are the signature of the memory-leak fix this sample encodes); descq_cnt is a u16 descriptor-FIFO depth; curr_head walks the head-writeback DMA region; ppd is computed immediately as dd->pport + port (pointer arithmetic indexed by the caller's port); per_sdma_credits is a computed credit budget; idle_cnt is seeded from the sdma_idle_cnt module parameter; and num_engines is seeded from the hardware via chip_sdma_engines(dd) as a size_t. num_engines is the master count that drives every subsequent allocation and both loops.

A capability gate: if the SDMA feature is not enabled in the kernel capability set, the routine clears the dependent SDMA_AHG capability and returns success (0) without allocating anything. This is a clean early-out that leaves dd->per_sdma NULL, which callers and teardown must tolerate.

The mod_num_sdma module parameter can override the hardware-derived engine count, but only if it is non-zero, does not exceed the chip's engine count, and is at least the number of virtual lanes (num_vls). This is an admin-tunable override with sane clamping — it cannot force num_engines above what the chip reports, and it cannot go below num_vls. Note it can still be exactly num_vls (>=1 in practice) but the clamp does not itself defend a downstream divide-by-count.

After logging the resolved counts, per_sdma_credits is computed as the chip's total SDMA memory size divided by (num_engines * SDMA_BLOCK_SIZE). This apportions the on-chip SDMA memory evenly across the engines. The divisor is a product of num_engines (from hardware or the clamped module param) and the fixed SDMA_BLOCK_SIZE macro; the result is a u32 credit budget later handed to init_sdma_regs. This is the one arithmetic statement whose safety depends on num_engines being non-zero.

The freeze/unfreeze synchronization primitives are initialized (a waitqueue and an atomic counter used when the device is quiesced). descq_cnt is then obtained from sdma_get_descq_cnt() into a u16 — this is the per-engine descriptor FIFO depth and is expected to be a power of two (it is later fed to ilog2 and used to build a mask). The counts are logged for diagnostics.

The array of num_engines sdma_engine structures is allocated with kcalloc_node (zeroing, NUMA-node-local, overflow-checked internally by kcalloc). On failure it returns -ENOMEM directly. Because this is the first allocation, an early failure here needs no cleanup, so returning without goto bail is correct. dd->per_sdma is the backing store both loops index.

idle_cnt (from the sdma_idle_cnt module param) is converted from nanoseconds to core-clock ticks. If non-zero, the default descriptor-1 template is set to request head-writeback-to-host (interrupt coalescing by idle timeout); otherwise it requests a completion interrupt per descriptor. sdma_desct_intr, if left at zero, is defaulted to SDMA_DESC_INTR. These configure interrupt/coalescing policy for the engines.

The main per-engine initialization loop. For each engine index it wires back-pointers (dd, ppd, this_idx), records descq_cnt, and seeds desc_avail from sdma_descq_freecnt(sde). It derives sdma_shift = ilog2(descq_cnt) and sdma_mask = (1 << sdma_shift) - 1, i.e. a ring-index mask assuming descq_cnt is a power of two. It then builds three per-source interrupt bit masks (int/progress/idle) by shifting 1 into position this_idx within stride TXE_NUM_SDMA_ENGINES, and ORs them into a combined imask. These masks assume this_idx stays below TXE_NUM_SDMA_ENGINES so the three sources do not collide.

The engine's concurrency and lifecycle objects are initialized: five locks (tail/head/senddmactrl/flushlist/waitlock, a mix of spinlocks and seqlocks), the AHG-bits accumulator preset so a zero bit always exists, and the state machine set to s00_hw_down. Reference counting is established via kref_init and a completion. The flushlist and dmawait list heads are initialized. tail_csr is resolved to this engine's TAIL CSR MMIO address. Two tasklets (hardware and software cleanup) and two workqueue items (err_halt, flush) are registered, and an error-progress-check timer is armed via timer_setup. This is where the engine acquires callable state — kref and the tasklets/workers are the lifetime machinery the teardown path must unwind.

Each engine gets two heap objects. descq is a coherent DMA buffer of descq_cnt * sizeof(u64[2]) bytes (each hardware descriptor is two u64 words) with its bus address stored in descq_phys. tx_ring is a software array of descq_cnt sdma_txreq pointers, allocated with kvzalloc_node using array_size() (the overflow-saturating multiply helper). Any allocation failure jumps to bail, which is the shared cleanup label; note that at that point earlier engines and the current engine's already-allocated descq are live and must be reclaimed by sdma_clean. There is a deliberate asymmetry: tx_ring's size uses the overflow-safe array_size() while descq's size uses a plain multiply.

After the engines, a single coherent DMA region of L1_CACHE_BYTES * num_engines bytes is allocated to hold hardware head-register writebacks (one cacheline per engine so engines do not false-share). sdma_heads_size records that size for later teardown. A small 4-byte pad DMA buffer is also allocated. Both failures jump to bail. sdma_heads_size is stored on dd, so teardown can free exactly this region.

The second loop carves the head-writeback region into per-engine cachelines: curr_head starts at the region base and advances by L1_CACHE_BYTES each iteration, giving each engine its own head_dma virtual pointer and corresponding head_phys bus address (base bus address plus the same byte offset). It then programs the engine's hardware registers via init_sdma_regs with the previously computed per_sdma_credits and idle_cnt. Because it walks exactly num_engines cachelines and the region was sized as L1_CACHE_BYTES * num_engines, curr_head stays within the allocation.

Device flags are set to advertise SendDMA (and the timeout variant if idle_cnt is non-zero), and dd->num_sdma records the engine count. sdma_map_init builds the VL-to-engine routing map; on failure it bails. The final resource is the sdma_rht hashtable: it is allocated into the LOCAL tmp_sdma_rht, then rhashtable_init is attempted. Crucially, if kzalloc fails or rhashtable_init returns an error, tmp_sdma_rht is kfree'd before bailing, and dd->sdma_rht is only published (line 179) AFTER rhashtable_init succeeds. This is exactly the memory-leak fix the sample name encodes: the previous buggy form assigned dd->sdma_rht up front (or failed to free the allocation), leaking the rhashtable object when init failed. On full success it returns 0.

The single error-cleanup label calls sdma_clean(dd, num_engines) and returns the saved errno. sdma_clean is expected to iterate all num_engines entries and free whatever was allocated (descq DMA buffers, tx_ring arrays, the head/pad DMA regions, the per_sdma array, kill tasklets/timers, drop krefs). The correctness of every goto bail above depends on sdma_clean tolerating partially-initialized engines (e.g. an engine whose descq succeeded but whose tx_ring was NULL) without double-freeing or dereferencing NULL.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This division is unguarded against num_engines == 0. num_engines is set from chip_sdma_engines(dd) (line 12) or from the clamped mod_num_sdma (line 24). Nothing between line 12 and line 32 rejects a zero engine count: the mod_num_sdma clamp only applies when mod_num_sdma is non-zero, so if the chip reports zero engines num_engines stays 0. A zero divisor here is an integer divide-by-zero, which on most architectures raises a fault and panics the kernel (CWE-369). The safe invariant is that num_engines must be validated as >= 1 (ideally >= num_vls) before this division; the module-parameter path enforces a lower bound but the hardware path does not. Realistic trigger requires degenerate/mis-programmed or emulated hardware, so severity is bounded, but it is the sharpest latent defect in the shown code.

descq_cnt comes from sdma_get_descq_cnt() (line 38) and is assumed to be a non-zero power of two. If it were 0, ilog2(0) is undefined (the kernel ilog2 of a runtime 0 yields an undefined/garbage shift), and (1 << shift) - 1 would produce a bogus ring mask; if it were not a power of two, sdma_mask would not correspond to descq_cnt and ring index wrapping (index & sdma_mask) could exceed the descq/tx_ring bounds (CWE-682 / potential CWE-125/787 in the fast path that uses the mask). The implementation must guarantee descq_cnt is a non-zero power of two before this point. This is a contract on sdma_get_descq_cnt(), not on attacker input, so it is latent/low.

This is the memory-leak (CWE-401) contract that names the sample. In this FIXED form the object is held in a local (tmp_sdma_rht) and freed with kfree on the rhashtable_init failure path before goto bail, and dd->sdma_rht is only published after success — so no leak occurs. The historical/buggy pattern this replaces assigned dd->sdma_rht = kzalloc(...) directly and, when rhashtable_init failed, jumped to bail without freeing it (and sdma_clean did not free sdma_rht), permanently leaking the allocation on every probe failure. What to confirm in the implementation: that sdma_clean does NOT also try to free/destroy dd->sdma_rht (which is still NULL on this path) — otherwise the fix would introduce a NULL destroy — and that the teardown that runs on later device removal calls rhashtable_free_and_destroy on the now-published dd->sdma_rht exactly once.

Every failure between the first per-engine allocation and the end funnels here with a partially-initialized engine array: e.g. an engine whose dma_alloc_coherent(descq) succeeded but whose tx_ring kvzalloc failed (lines 114-124), or a mid-loop failure leaving engines [0..k] fully built and engine k+1 half-built. sdma_clean(dd, num_engines) is called with the FULL num_engines, not the number actually initialized, so it must be written to tolerate NULL descq/tx_ring/head_dma and un-run tasklets/timers on the not-yet-reached engines without double-free, NULL-deref (CWE-476), or freeing DMA buffers that were never allocated. This is a latent contract in sdma_clean (file not provided): the loop bound passed here makes correct NULL-guarding in the cleanup function mandatory.

The descq allocation size is computed with a plain multiply, descq_cnt * sizeof(u64[2]), whereas the very next allocation (tx_ring, line 120) deliberately uses the overflow-saturating array_size() helper. With descq_cnt a u16 (max 65535) times 16 bytes the product tops out around 1 MiB and cannot overflow size_t, so there is no live overflow (CWE-190) here — but the inconsistency is a latent hazard: if a future change widened descq_cnt or the element size, the descq path would silently wrap while tx_ring stayed safe. Low severity; worth normalizing to array_size for defense in depth.

The caller-supplied u8 port is used directly as an index (pointer offset) into dd->pport with no bound check in this function. If port ever exceeds dd->num_pports - 1, ppd points outside the port array and later uses (sde->ppd = ppd at line 63, ppd->vls_operational at line 163) are out-of-bounds reads/writes (CWE-125/787). In practice sdma_init is called from the driver's own probe path with a validated port, so this is a caller contract rather than an exploitable bug here; the invariant to confirm is that every caller passes port < dd->num_pports.

summary: This is the FIXED revision of hfi1 sdma_init(); it is device/port bring-up code with no untrusted remote input path, so the residual risk is robustness, not remote memory corruption. The named issue (CWE-401 rhashtable leak) is correctly repaired by holding the object in a local, freeing it on rhashtable_init failure, and publishing dd->sdma_rht only on success. The remaining observations are latent and low: an unguarded divide-by-num_engines (CWE-369) if the chip reports zero engines, an ilog2/power-of-two assumption on descq_cnt, and the requirement that sdma_clean safely unwind partially-initialized engines.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Init-time signature; dd is trusted device context and port is a driver-supplied index — no untrusted data crosses here (see port anchor).
u8 port used as unchecked pointer index into dd->pport; caller contract that could cause OOB access if violated.
Master count; if 0 it drives a divide-by-zero at line 32 — enumerated under the per_sdma_credits finding.
Assumed non-zero power of two; ilog2(descq_cnt) and the derived sdma_mask break otherwise.
Clean capability gate returning 0 with nothing allocated; leaves per_sdma NULL which teardown must already tolerate — no contract violated here.
Root-only module param with upper (<= chip engines) and lower (>= num_vls) bounds; cannot exceed hardware. It does not, however, guard the zero case when the hardware count is used.
Unguarded division by num_engines — divide-by-zero if engine count is 0.
kcalloc is internally overflow-checked and the immediate NULL check returns before any cleanup is needed; correct.
Pure policy configuration from a root module param; no memory-safety contract.
Ring-mask derivation assumes descq_cnt is a non-zero power of two; undefined/incorrect mask otherwise.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: int/progress/idle interrupt masks with this_idx and TXE_NUM_SDMA_ENGINES (lines 71-79)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Bit positions are correct as long as this_idx < TXE_NUM_SDMA_ENGINES, which is enforced by the mod_num_sdma <= chip engines clamp; no attacker input.
Constant AHG-bit accumulator preset; no external input, no contract.
Lifecycle objects; their teardown correctness is captured by the sdma_clean partial-init finding rather than as a separate issue.
Plain multiply for allocation size (no array_size) — no live overflow because descq_cnt is u16, but flagged as an inconsistency/latent hazard.
Uses overflow-saturating array_size(); the goto bail on failure is handled by the sdma_clean partial-init contract.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: dd->sdma_heads_size = L1_CACHE_BYTES * num_engines and second head-carving loop (lines 127-159)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Region sized exactly L1_CACHE_BYTES * num_engines and the loop advances curr_head num_engines times; stays in bounds. Product cannot overflow for realistic num_engines.
Return value checked and bails on error; ppd->vls_operational safety depends on the port anchor already flagged.
The central CWE-401 leak contract; correctly handled in this fixed revision, documented with what to confirm in teardown.
Shared cleanup called with full num_engines against a possibly partially-initialized array — imposes a NULL-tolerance/no-double-free contract on sdma_clean.

findings.title: rhashtable (sdma_rht) memory-leak contract — correctly fixed via local + kfree-on-failure
findings.anchor: tmp_sdma_rht = kzalloc(...); if (rhashtable_init(...) < 0) kfree(tmp_sdma_rht); dd->sdma_rht = tmp_sdma_rht;
findings.bug_class: Memory leak on error path (missing kfree of allocated hashtable)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0235_cwe401.c (this is the fixed revision); teardown counterpart in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: The rhashtable object allocated by kzalloc must be freed on every failure path before it is published to dd->sdma_rht, and dd->sdma_rht must only be set after rhashtable_init succeeds so that later teardown frees it exactly once and never frees an un-published NULL.
findings.failure_mode: In the pre-fix form, dd->sdma_rht was assigned the allocation up front (or the kzalloc'd object was not freed) and a rhashtable_init failure jumped to bail without releasing it, leaking sizeof(struct rhashtable) on every probe failure — a slow kernel memory leak triggerable by repeated failed device init. This fixed form holds the object in the local tmp_sdma_rht, kfree's it on rhashtable_init failure, and publishes dd->sdma_rht only on success, so no leak occurs.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm sdma_clean() (called at bail) does NOT attempt to destroy/free dd->sdma_rht, since on this failure path dd->sdma_rht is still NULL; and confirm the device-removal teardown calls rhashtable_free_and_destroy on the published dd->sdma_rht exactly once.

findings.title: Unguarded division by num_engines (divide-by-zero if chip reports zero engines)
findings.invariant: num_engines must be validated as non-zero (and in practice >= num_vls) before being used as a divisor; the mod_num_sdma clamp enforces this only when the module override is active, not on the hardware-derived path.
findings.failure_mode: num_engines is seeded from chip_sdma_engines(dd) (line 12) and only overridden by mod_num_sdma when that is non-zero and within bounds. If the chip (or an emulated/faulty device) reports 0 engines, num_engines stays 0, and num_engines * SDMA_BLOCK_SIZE is 0, causing an integer divide-by-zero at line 32 that faults and panics the kernel during init.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm chip_sdma_engines(dd) can never return 0 for supported hardware, or add an explicit 'if (!num_engines) goto bail/return -EINVAL' before line 31.

findings.title: sdma_clean called with full num_engines over a possibly partially-initialized engine array
findings.bug_class: Cleanup of partially-initialized objects (NULL-deref / double-free risk in teardown)
findings.invariant: sdma_clean must iterate all num_engines entries tolerating NULL descq/tx_ring/head_dma and un-run tasklets/timers/krefs (from engines the loop never reached or half-built), freeing each resource at most once and never dereferencing a field that was never initialized.
findings.failure_mode: Any goto bail from within the engine loop (lines 118/124) or after it leaves engines [k+1..num_engines-1] zero-initialized (kcalloc-zeroed) and engine k possibly half-built (descq allocated, tx_ring NULL). If sdma_clean assumes every engine is fully initialized, it will NULL-deref or double-free during error cleanup, turning an allocation failure into a kernel crash or corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify sdma_clean NULL-checks sde->descq, sde->tx_ring, sde->head_dma before freeing and safely handles tasklet/timer/kref objects for engines that were never fully brought up.

findings.title: descq_cnt assumed non-zero power of two for ilog2 and ring mask
findings.bug_class: Incorrect calculation from unvalidated power-of-two assumption
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0235_cwe401.c; producer sdma_get_descq_cnt() in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: descq_cnt (from sdma_get_descq_cnt) must be a non-zero power of two so that sdma_mask == descq_cnt - 1 exactly bounds ring indices used later as (index & sdma_mask).
findings.failure_mode: If descq_cnt were 0, ilog2(0) is undefined and yields a garbage shift; if it were not a power of two, sdma_mask would not equal descq_cnt - 1, so later fast-path ring indexing (index & sdma_mask) could address descq/tx_ring slots beyond descq_cnt, an out-of-bounds access. Not attacker-controlled here, hence low, but it is an unenforced correctness contract at init.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm sdma_get_descq_cnt() always returns a non-zero power of two (e.g. clamped/rounded), or add an explicit WARN/validation.

findings.title: descq allocation size uses plain multiply instead of overflow-safe array_size (latent, inconsistent with tx_ring)
findings.invariant: Allocation byte sizes derived from a count times an element size should saturate/reject on overflow (as the adjacent tx_ring does via array_size()).
findings.failure_mode: No live overflow today: descq_cnt is u16 (<=65535) and sizeof(u64[2]) is 16, so the product is <=~1 MiB and fits size_t. The hazard is latent/consistency: unlike the very next allocation (tx_ring uses array_size at line 120), this multiply is unguarded, so widening descq_cnt or the element size in a future change could wrap it into a small allocation and later overflow the descq buffer.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm descq_cnt can never exceed u16 range and, for defense in depth, switch to array_size(descq_cnt, sizeof(u64[2])).

findings.title: Caller-supplied u8 port used as unchecked index into dd->pport
findings.bug_class: Unvalidated array index (out-of-bounds access if contract violated)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0235_cwe401.c; callers in implementation (file not provided)
findings.invariant: Every caller must pass port < dd->num_pports so that dd->pport + port stays within the allocated port array before it is stored (sde->ppd, line 63) and dereferenced (ppd->vls_operational, line 163).
findings.failure_mode: port is a u8 and is not bound-checked in this function; if a caller ever passed a port >= dd->num_pports, ppd would point outside the port array and subsequent reads/writes through it would be out-of-bounds. In the current driver sdma_init is invoked from the probe path with a validated port, so this is a caller contract rather than a live bug.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm all call sites of sdma_init pass a port already validated against dd->num_pports.

audit_checklist: Confirm chip_sdma_engines(dd) cannot return 0 (or add a zero-guard before the per_sdma_credits division at line 32) to prevent a divide-by-zero panic.

Verify sdma_clean(dd, num_engines) NULL-tolerates partially-initialized engines: NULL-check descq, tx_ring, head_dma and safely handle un-run tasklets/timers/krefs, freeing each resource at most once.
Confirm dd->sdma_rht is only freed once, in device-removal teardown, and that sdma_clean does not attempt to destroy the still-NULL dd->sdma_rht on the rhashtable_init failure path.
Confirm sdma_get_descq_cnt() always yields a non-zero power of two so ilog2(descq_cnt) and sdma_mask correctly bound ring indexing.
Confirm descq_cnt stays within u16 range and consider array_size() for the descq allocation (line 115) to match the overflow-safe tx_ring allocation.
Confirm all sdma_init callers pass port < dd->num_pports so dd->pport + port is in-bounds.
Confirm the DMA regions (descq, sdma_heads_dma, sdma_pad_dma) allocated here are freed on the matching teardown with the same sizes recorded (dd->sdma_heads_size) to avoid leaks/mismatched frees.
